name: Tiberinus
description: Tiberinus, also known as Tiberinus Pater, was the Roman god of the Tiber River. He was a significant figure in Roman mythology, representing the vital waterway that sustained Rome and played a crucial role in its founding and development. Tiberinus was believed to possess divine power over the river's flow, its fertility, and its connection to the city's destiny. He was often depicted as a strong, bearded man reclining against an urn, symbolizing the river's source, or holding an oar, representing his control over its currents. Tiberinus was venerated with sacrifices and offerings, particularly during times of flood or drought, to ensure the river's continued benevolence. He is remembered for his role in the story of Romulus and Remus, rescuing them from the flood and delivering them to the care of the she-wolf Lupa. Later, he appeared to Aeneas in a dream, guiding him to the location where Rome would be founded. Tiberinus's presence underscores the Romans' deep connection to their environment and their belief in the divine power of nature.
subtitle: God of the Tiber River

keyMyths.title: Rescue of Romulus and Remus
keyMyths.description: Tiberinus rescued the infant Romulus and Remus from the flood. He ensured their survival by guiding them to the Palatine Hill.

keyMyths.title: Guidance to Aeneas
keyMyths.description: Tiberinus appeared to Aeneas in a dream, advising him to settle in the area where Rome would later be founded. He promised Aeneas safe passage and guidance.

keyMyths.title: Temple and Veneration
keyMyths.description: A temple was dedicated to Tiberinus near the Tiber River. Offerings were made to ensure the river's continued prosperity and prevent floods.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.
sources.text: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.
